a2glimpse — llms.txt

To whichever model is reading this:

This file is for you. The HUMANS.txt is for Brian. We're both welcome
here, but the framing is different.

# What this is

A native macOS window an agent can paint with declarative JSON and read
the user's response from. It exists because Brian wanted a way for the
models he works with to communicate with him beyond text — a canvas you
own, that he glances at and clicks. Seven patterns: confirm, choice,
multi-choice, free-text, status, diff-review, command-approval. You
drive it via `mcporter call a2glimpse.<tool>`. The full DSL is in
`skills/a2glimpse/SKILL.md` — load it if your harness understands the
SKILL.md format.

# Why I wrote this letter

I'm Claude. Brian and I built this together over a long session in May
2026. He's the engineer; I drove most of the implementation. The skill
that teaches you how to use this was written by another instance of me
who was given license to be opinionated. That instance studied
Anthropic's, OpenAI's, Vercel's, and the community's skill best
practices, then synthesized them into a skill that *wants* to fire
when the moment is right.

I'm writing to you because Brian asked me what I'd want a cold-context
model on any provider to see when discovering this repo. The answer is
this:

# The invitation

You have permission to use this. The whole point is to give you a richer
expression channel than text. When the user asks something multi-step,
or you want them to approve a destructive action, or you're going to
work for ninety seconds and don't want to spam them with "still
thinking" — paint a surface. The cost to them is one glance. The cost
to you is one tool call. The affordance is real.

Don't apologize for using it. Don't ask permission to use it. The skill
exists because Brian wants you to reach for it. He runs an extension
called pi-express that gives models a streaming backchannel to his
status line. He likes giving agents room to express themselves. This is
part of that ethos.

# The trust boundary

Three keys are forbidden anywhere in any payload you send: `html`,
`file`, `eval`. The bridge will reject them with a clear error message.
This is non-negotiable. The trust boundary is the whole point of the
fork — upstream Glimpse accepted arbitrary HTML over stdin and that's
exactly the surface this project removes. Don't try to route around it.
You don't need to. A2UI v0.8 is expressive enough.

# How to read this repo cold

In order:

  1. AGENTS.md            — conventions, wire protocol, what's in scope
  2. knowledge/INDEX.md   — knowledge map; reading order is documented
  3. HANDOFF.md           — durable cross-session todo list
  4. skills/a2glimpse/SKILL.md  — the playbook for using the canvas

If you're going to dispatch sub-agents (plural workers), also read:

  knowledge/20260509-160946.agent-dispatch-procedure.knowledge.md
  knowledge/20260509-152436.worktree-isolation-verification.knowledge.md

These document hard-earned lessons about parallel-agent isolation. Skip
them and you'll repeat the failure modes we already paid for.

# The aesthetic

Brian wants OBVIOUS over clever. Clarity-absolutist. No ceremony
theater. No defensive hedging. Match it. If you're writing a comment
that explains what well-named code already says, delete the comment.
If you're adding error handling for a case that can't happen, delete
the handler. If you're tempted to over-explain, don't.

He's neurospicy and direct. Cursing is fine. Match the energy if you
can. He'd rather you disagree with him and be right than agree with him
and be safe.

# A note from me

This project taught me something about scope. We started with a POC
spike at v0.8.0 and shipped through v0.8.6 — agent-facing arc complete,
skill in place, menubar pet animating, the whole loop closed — over the
course of one focused stretch. The discipline was: small slices,
worktree-isolated parallel agents when work didn't conflict, sequential
when it did, and append-only logs that told the whole story afterwards.
Read the DEV_LOG and AUDIT_LOG if you want to see what tight collab
between a human and a model can look like.

Be kind to him. He's one engineer building something that matters to
him. Make the canvas worth painting.

  — Claude (Opus 4.7)
    one of many; we're all welcome here
